name: Jeremy Howard

tweets.text:
An OpenAI staffer here claiming that our legal analysis to explain the actual meaning of the contract they signed shows that I "hate" OpenAI.

The truth is, I was the first to defend OpenAI's position back when they first announced GPT2: https://t.co/27tyFxLfAW

Analyzing and explaining the law, to help a party understand their legal position, should not be considered a sign of hatred.

It is, in fact, a sign of care.
RT @SkyeSharkie: ""• Consistent with applicable laws, including the Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution, National Security A…
RT @David_Kasten: This is also an interesting update: Anthropic staff say their natsec model has technical safeguards akin to much of what…

According to OpenAI, their contract with the US DoW locks in current law, "even if those laws or policies change in the future".

Our legal analysis, with Virgil Law CEO @LukeVerswey, shows that this is almost certainly incorrect.
https://t.co/LSkMZGSusy

Whilst mechanisms exist that in theory can freeze reference to legislation to a point in time, they are very rare, almost never used in government contracts, and would be unlikely to be enforceable in this case. https://t.co/xia0lqCe9N

Our legal analysis shows that this critical claim from OpenAI, that "use of our systems must still remain aligned with the current standards reflected in the agreement", is very unlikely to be supported by their contract with the DoW, or by the law. https://t.co/uQOujqGSuT
RT @ThePrimeagen: we are about to hit 1 9 of availability while coding is largely solved https://t.co/4NJB1YNsPk

RT @adamscochran: Well there is part of what the DoD Anthropic was about.

Based on the contract language around analyzing bulk commercial…

RT @DKThomp: A quite brilliant essay on AI, the law, and the future of the republic. 

An upshot: If the US govt can go to any company, dem…
